title: Right and Wrong in AI

modules.title: What Makes an AI Decision Ethical?
modules.sub: Exploring the values and principles behind AI choices
modules.description: Learners examine what it means for an AI system to make a decision and why not all decisions are morally neutral. By applying foundational ethical frameworks, they begin to build a personal lens for evaluating AI behavior in real-world contexts.

modules.title: Who Builds AI and Why It Matters
modules.sub: Tracing how builder identity shapes AI outcomes
modules.description: Learners investigate who designs, funds, and deploys AI systems and how those actors' incentives influence what gets built. They reflect on how power, perspective, and profit can embed assumptions into technology long before it reaches users.

modules.title: Fairness: More Than Equal Treatment
modules.sub: Unpacking competing definitions of fairness in AI
modules.description: Learners explore why fairness is not a single standard and how different definitions can produce contradictory outcomes in practice. Through case studies in hiring, lending, and criminal justice, they weigh the trade-offs each fairness model demands.

modules.title: Privacy in the Age of AI
modules.sub: Understanding what AI systems collect, infer, and reveal
modules.description: Learners map the many ways AI systems gather and exploit personal data, often far beyond what users expect or consent to. They assess real examples of surveillance, profiling, and inference to determine where meaningful privacy boundaries should lie.

modules.title: When Algorithms Cause Real Harm
modules.sub: Connecting automated decisions to human consequences
modules.description: Learners analyse documented cases where algorithmic systems produced discrimination, physical risk, or psychological damage. They practice identifying the chain of design choices, incentives, and oversights that allowed harm to occur and scale.

modules.title: Accountability: Who Is Responsible?
modules.sub: Assigning responsibility when AI systems go wrong
modules.description: Learners work through scenarios where AI causes harm and attempt to locate responsibility across developers, deployers, regulators, and users. They grapple with how distributed AI supply chains deliberately or inadvertently obscure clear lines of accountability.

modules.title: Designing Ethics Into AI Systems
modules.sub: From abstract principles to concrete design decisions
modules.description: Learners step into the role of a design reviewer and evaluate how ethical principles can be operationalised in system architecture, data practices, and testing protocols. They identify where ethical commitments are most likely to erode under business or technical pressure.

modules.title: Your Role as an Ethical AI User
modules.sub: Acting with intention and influence in an AI world
modules.description: Learners synthesise the course by identifying the concrete choices available to them as individuals — from how they use AI tools to how they advocate, report, and push back. They leave with a personal action plan grounded in the ethical reasoning built across all prior modules.

synopsis: This course introduces the core ethical questions every person should ask about artificial intelligence, from fairness and privacy to accountability and harm. No technical background required — just curiosity and a willingness to think critically about the technology shaping our world.
rationale: With a demand signal of 7/10 and a corpus similarity of only 0.63 against a nearest match focused on abstract philosophical ethics, there is a clear gap for a grounded, accessible course that connects everyday AI interactions to real ethical stakes.
